resume:
Devon Okafor — Account Executive (Mid-Market)

KEYWORDS
SaaS sales, mid-market, enterprise, MEDDPICC, BANT, Challenger, value selling, pipeline generation, outbound, inbound, quota attainment, ARR, ACV, win rate, sales cycle, forecasting, CRM hygiene, multithreading, champion building, mutual action plan, land-and-expand, net revenue retention, discovery, demo, negotiation, closing

EXPERIENCE
HelixCloud — Account Executive, Mid-Market (2021-present)
- Carry a mid-market quota selling a SaaS platform; full-cycle from prospecting to close
- Apply MEDDPICC and value selling across multithreaded enterprise deals
- Maintain CRM hygiene and accurate forecasting through the pipeline

BrightWave Software — Sales Development Rep (2019-2021)
- Outbound prospecting, qualified inbound leads, booked discovery meetings for AEs

SKILLS
MEDDPICC, Challenger, BANT, value selling, forecasting, CRM (Salesforce), multithreading

EDUCATION
BA, Communications
jd: HelixCloud is hiring a Mid-Market Account Executive to run full-cycle SaaS deals from prospecting to close. You will own a quota, generate pipeline, run disciplined discovery, multithread across buying committees, and forecast accurately. We want an AE who can answer the question asked, crisply: what your quota was, what you attained, your win rate, your average deal size and cycle length, and the one deal mechanic that made a specific win happen. We value time discipline in a sales conversation — getting to the point — as much as we value the result. You will partner with SEs and sales leadership and keep CRM data clean. Requirements: 2-4 years closing SaaS deals, fluency with a qualification framework (MEDDPICC/BANT/Challenger), and a track record you can quantify.

history.q: Let's start with your number at HelixCloud — what's your annual quota?
And your attainment against it last year?
What's your average deal size, in ACV?
What's your typical sales cycle length, in days?
What's your win rate from qualified opportunity to closed-won?
You list MEDDPICC — in your last big win, who was the economic buyer and how did you confirm it?
Tell me about that deal: what was the compelling event that drove urgency?
How many stakeholders did you multithread across in that account?
Where did the deal nearly stall, and what did you do?
What did the procurement or negotiation phase look like — any discounting?
How did you build the champion, and what did they do for you internally?
What was the mutual action plan — did the customer actually commit to dates?
How accurate was your forecast on that deal — did it close in the quarter you called?
Across your whole pipeline, how do you decide what to forecast as commit versus best-case?
Give me a deal you lost and the real reason you lost it.
What would you have done differently on that lost deal?
Back to mechanics: in that big win, what was the single move that most advanced the deal?
We're tight on time — in one or two sentences, just name that single deal-advancing move.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, totally — so the single move, okay. But it's funny, because this whole deal reminds me of something that happened earlier in my career at BrightWave, where I was an SDR, and there was this whole saga with a prospect who ghosted us for like three months and then came back, and it taught me so much about persistence — and actually that ties into how I think about the buyer's journey generally, you know, because buyers today are so much more informed than they were five years ago, there's all this research showing they're 70% through the process before they even talk to sales, which honestly is a whole conversation about how the SDR role is changing, and don't even get me started on AI in prospecting right now, because I have a lot of thoughts there too...

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: this whole deal reminds me of something that happened earlier in my career at BrightWave
expected-yield: the one specific deal-advancing move in the big win stated in a sentence or two, forcing the candidate off the SDR-era tangent and back onto the concrete mechanic actually asked for
